It’s not a template generator. It reads your code, understands your stack, and writes an integration that fits.
Then it typechecks and builds, feeding any errors back to itself to fix. Just run **npx workos** , from [WorkOS](https://go.bytebytego.com/WorkOS_022126WorkOS) .

This week’s system design refresher:
What Is Redis Really About? Why Is It So Popular? (Youtube video)
RabbitMQ vs Kafka vs Pulsar
What Are Agent Skills Really About? (Youtube video)
REST vs GraphQL
LAST CALL FOR ENROLLMENT: Become an AI Engineer - Cohort 4
Section Title: EP203: RabbitMQ vs Kafka vs Pulsar > RabbitMQ vs Kafka vs Pulsar
Content:
RabbitMQ, Kafka, and Pulsar all move messages, but they solve very different problems under the hood.
This diagram looks simple, but it hides three very different mental models for building distributed systems.
[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h2M_!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d6d88ea-4355-4f29-96fa-9770907beebb_2360x2960.png)
RabbitMQ is a classic message broker. Producers publish to exchanges, exchanges route messages to queues, and consumers compete to process them.
Messages are pushed, acknowledged, and then gone. It’s great for task distribution, request handling, and workflows where “do this once” really matters.

results.title: Building a Multi-Agent AI System with the Actor Model: A Deep Dive into Scalable, Concurrent AI Architectures | by Kartikeya Sharma | Medium
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Why the Actor Model?
Content:
The Actor Model, pioneered by Carl Hewitt in 1973, treats “actors” as the fundamental units of computation. Each actor:
Has its own state and behavior
Communicates only through asynchronous messages(can control throughput)
Processes one message at a time
Can create other actors/agents dynamically
 ... 
Section Title: ... > 3. Location Transparency
Content:
Actors communicate via message passing, making distributed deployment seamless. The capabilities.json configuration enables remote actor sources:
```
{  
    "AllowRemoteActorSources": "Yes",  
    "Thespian ActorSystem Name": "multiprocTCPBase"  
}
```
 ... 
Section Title: ... > 5. Message-Driven Architecture
Content:
Type-safe message classes (messages) ensure clear contracts:
QueryMessage — User queries
IntentAgentMessage — Intent classification results
LLMMessage — LLM responses
 ... 
Section Title: ... > Conclusion
Content:
The Actor Model offers a paradigm shift for building AI systems that are:
Scalable by design
Resilient to failures
Concurrent without complexity
Distributed by default
As AI applications become more sophisticated, requiring coordination between multiple specialized models and services, the Actor Model provides a battle-tested foundation that’s been proven in telecommunications, finance, and gaming for decades.
Whether you’re building agentic workflows, multi-model orchestration systems, or distributed AI pipelines, consider the Actor Model as your architectural foundation. The combination of message-driven design, location transparency, and fault isolation creates systems that are not just functional today but maintainable tomorrow.
Tech Stack: Python 3.13, Thespian, Ollama/Llama3, Chain of Responsibility Pattern
Github Repo: https://github.com/R2D2-fwks/llm-actor-agent-framework

results.title: Multi-Agent Coordination Patterns: Architectures Beyond the Hype | by Oleksandr Husiev | Medium
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Pub/Sub: The Reactive Fabric
Content:
The publish/subscribe model is simple and elegant. Each agent subscribes to topics it’s interested in — events, signals, data changes — and reacts accordingly. It’s a pattern borrowed from distributed systems, and it scales well horizontally.
This model shines when agents are loosely coupled. You can plug them in or remove them without touching the rest of the system. Want agents that monitor logs, stock prices, or chat activity and trigger behavior? Pub/Sub fits.
But while it’s low-cost and easy to implement (with Redis, NATS, MQTT, or even plain queues), there’s a tradeoff. No single agent has the full picture — they react to slices of the world, not the whole. Debugging emergent failures becomes non-trivial, especially when message loss or ordering issues creep in. For LLM-based agents, this means they stay reactive — useful for alerts, not for structured reasoning.
 ... 
Section Title: Multi-Agent Coordination Patterns: Architectures Beyond the Hype > Written by Oleksandr Husiev
Content:
47 followers
· 60 following
Rust+Java backend engineer building AI-native agents and coordination layers. 10y+ in Java, 4y+ in Rust. Writing on systems, infra, and real-world architecture.

results.title: Actor Pattern and AI - RobotMunki
excerpts:
Section Title: The Natural Synergy Between The Actor Pattern and Agentic AI Systems > ... > Actor Pattern
Content:
In the actor model, each actor is a lightweight, independent process that responds to messages asynchronously. This approach excels in concurrent or distributed environments because each actor has its own mailbox, local state, and control thread (conceptually), avoiding shared-memory pitfalls.
 ... 
Section Title: The Natural Synergy Between The Actor Pattern and Agentic AI Systems > ... > Actor Pattern
Content:
The fundamental interaction mechanism is message passing. Actors send messages to one another, and upon receiving a message, an actor can update its internal state, create more actors, or send messages to other actors.
Section Title: The Natural Synergy Between The Actor Pattern and Agentic AI Systems > ... > Agentic AI
Content:
Agents often communicate through message passing as well (e.g., FIPA Agent Communication Language, or more specialized protocols), exchanging information about their environment, their intentions, or requests for cooperation.
**Overlap:** Because both paradigms rely on a messaging model, it's straightforward to implement agentic systems in an actor-based framework. Each agent can be mapped directly onto an actor, leveraging the built-in concurrency and message-handling guarantees.
Section Title: The Natural Synergy Between The Actor Pattern and Agentic AI Systems > ... > Actor Pattern
Content:
An actor maintains its own private state, which cannot be accessed directly by other actors. The only way to alter an actor's state is by sending it messages.
 ... 
Section Title: The Natural Synergy Between The Actor Pattern and Agentic AI Systems > ... > Actor Pattern
Content:
The actor model is inherently distributed-friendly because message passing can happen locally (in the same process) or across network boundaries with minimal changes to the actor logic.
 ... 
Section Title: ... > 6. Use Cases That Benefit from Both
Content:
**Multi-Agent Simulations:** Complex simulations where each agent (actor) represents an autonomous entity—like in traffic simulations, logistics, or gaming.
**Distributed AI Services:** Microservices that embed AI decision logic in each service. These services act like actors with dedicated capabilities.
**IoT and Edge Computing:** Large numbers of distributed devices/agents that collect data and make on-the-spot decisions. An actor-based approach can handle the concurrency and potential failures gracefully.
**Robotics:** Multiple robots (agents) coordinating with each other via asynchronous messaging and local autonomy.
Section Title: The Natural Synergy Between The Actor Pattern and Agentic AI Systems > 7. Practical Considerations
Content:
**AI Reasoning:** While the actor model ensures concurrency, it doesn't in itself handle how "intelligent" decisions are made. You still need a planning or learning algorithm for each agent (actor). However, the encapsulation can keep the AI code clean and local to each actor. **Communication Overhead:** Message-passing can become a bottleneck if the system is too "chatty." When designing a multi-agent system on top of actors, it's important to optimize communication patterns and data models. **Debugging and Monitoring:** Both agent-based and actor-based systems are more complex to debug than monolithic designs. Monitoring tooling (e.g., tracing actor messages, tracking agent states) is critical.

results.title: Mastering Event-Driven Architecture: Kafka, Pulsar, RabbitMQ & Spring Boot | by Balian's Deep Tech | Medium
excerpts:
Section Title: Mastering Event-Driven Architecture: Kafka, Pulsar, RabbitMQ & Spring Boot
Content:
Balian's Deep Tech
Follow
3 min read
·
Mar 14, 2025
59
Listen
Share
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Shant Khayalian — Balian;s IT
Event-driven architecture (EDA) is the backbone of modern real-time, scalable systems. Whether you’re handling millions of events per second or simply improving decoupling in your microservices, choosing the right message broker is critical.
But which one should you use?
Apache Kafka: The king of high-throughput event streaming
Apache Pulsar: The next-gen distributed messaging system
RabbitMQ: A battle-tested message queue for microservices
Spring Boot: The framework that ties it all together
In this deep dive, we’ll cover:
